---
title: "Tidy data"
author: "Nicky Wakim"
title-slide-attributes:
    data-background-color: "#3070BF"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: "../simple_NW.scss"
    chalkboard: true
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    width: 1955
    height: 1100
    footer: "Tidy data"
    html-math-method: mathjax
    highlight-style: ayu
code-annotations: hover
execute:
  echo: true
  freeze: auto
---

```{r}
#| label: "setup" 
#| include: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

pacman::p_load(
  tidyverse, 
  lubridate,
  janitor,
  here, 
  rio, 
  readxl
)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
hrs_data_rds <- import(here("data", "hrs_data.rds"))
hrs_data <- clean_names(hrs_data_rds)
```

## Tidy data[^1]

[^1]: Source: R for Data Science. Grolemund and Wickham.

![](../img_slides/tidy-data-frame.png)

1.  Each variable must have its own column

2.  Each observation must have its own row

3.  Each value must have its own cell

## Excel data are often *not* tidy

- We might be used to seeing data in "human readability" form
- R cannot read this!

![[From The Epidemiologist R Handbook, Section 4.1](https://epirhandbook.com/en/new_pages/transition_to_R.html#tidy-data)](../img_slides/Excel_not_tidy.png)

## Let's look at an example[^2] of data represented in different ways

[^2]: Source: R for Data Science. Grolemund and Wickham. [Example pulled from Section 5.2](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/data-tidy.html#sec-tidy-data)

We will look at a dataset measuring tuberculosis (TB) cases for different countries. We have information on the country, year, population, and number of TB cases: 

::: columns
::: {.column width="30%"}
[**Each column is one piece of information:**]{style="color:#EF85B3;"}

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
table1
```
:::
:::
::: {.column width="38%"}
[**Population and cases are "types" of counts:**]{style="color:#5BAFF8;"}

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
table2
```
:::
:::
::: {.column width="30%"}
[**We look at the rate of TB cases:**]{style="color:#F39C12;"}

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
table3
```
:::
:::
:::

## Tidy data works best with R functions

- Tidy data allow us to transform, summarize, and plot our data very easily! 

::: columns
::: column
- **Example:** If we want to calculate the rate of TB for each country and year, we will be able to simply instruct R to calculate $$ \text{rate} = \dfrac{\text{cases}}{\text{population}}$$
:::
::: column
[**Each column is one piece of information:**]{style="color:#EF85B3;"}

```{r}
#| echo: false
table1
```
:::
:::

## Our HRS data are in tidy form

- Each column is a piece of information for an individual in the dataset

```{r}
tibble(hrs_data) |> print(n = 8)
```

## Wrap-up

- Tidy data are especially useful in R!
- We will see more of its advantages in later lessons

## Resources
